nativeRange.habitat: Still and sluggish freshwaters — rice paddies, swamps, roadside ditches, streams and ponds; Khorat Plateau in eastern Thailand and Mekong tributaries in central Laos

decorPreferences: dense planting with tall and floating plants
leaf litter and driftwood for tannins and cover
gentle sponge filtration — strong currents should be avoided
tight-fitting lid mandatory (labyrinth fish escape readily)

diet.notes: Micro-carnivore in the wild, feeding on aquatic and terrestrial invertebrates. In captivity, generally accepts quality micro-pellets and dried foods once acclimated, but tends to benefit from regular offerings of small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia, Artemia, bloodworm, and mosquito larvae. Small meals offered once or twice daily are generally preferred over a single large feeding.

goodWith: peaceful loaches in larger planted tanks
other Betta smaragdina pairs or females in well-planted, adequately sized tanks
avoidWith: Betta splendens (hybridizes readily — keep strictly separate)
other Splendens-complex wild Bettas without careful supervision

summary: Wild-form labyrinth fish from Thailand and Laos prized for stunning iridescent emerald-green body scaling. One of the Splendens complex species; less aggressive than B. splendens but still escape-prone. Bubble-nester with intermediate care needs.
careNotes:
Betta smaragdina is one of the 'Splendens complex' wild Betta species — closely related to B. splendens, B. imbellis, and B. mahachaiensis. It is prized by wild-Betta enthusiasts for its brilliant iridescent emerald-green and blue-green scale iridescence, which tends to be more vivid under subdued lighting against a dark substrate and leaf litter.

This species hybridizes freely with Betta splendens and possibly other Splendens-complex members. Wild-caught or specialist-bred stock should be kept strictly separate from B. splendens to preserve genetic integrity. Some specimens sold as 'Emerald Betta' in the trade may already carry B. splendens genetics; sourcing from reputable killifish-style specialist breeders is generally advised.

Males tend to be less aggressive toward each other than B. splendens, and multiple males are sometimes kept together in well-planted tanks with ample sight-line breaks — but this is not guaranteed and individual variation is significant. A pair in a 40L+ planted setup is the most reliable arrangement. Sorority-style groups of females with one male have also been reported to work in larger tanks.

Soft, slightly acidic water is generally preferred; this is a rice-paddy and lowland-swamp species from the Khorat Plateau. Peat filtration, Indian almond leaves (catappa), and driftwood all help recreate tannin-stained water conditions. Hard or strongly alkaline water tends to stress this species over time.

A heater is required — this is a tropical species. A tight-fitting lid is non-negotiable: labyrinth fish are strong jumpers, and small escape gaps are easily exploited during threat displays or at night. Surface access must remain unobstructed for atmospheric breathing via the labyrinth organ.

FishBase lists this species as 'Data Deficient' on the IUCN Red List (assessed 2011), which likely reflects limited field survey data rather than confirmed population security. Wild collection pressure and rice-paddy drainage are potential concerns in parts of its range.
breedingNotes: Bubble-nest builder with paternal care. The male constructs a floating nest of mucus-coated bubbles at the surface, typically anchored under a floating leaf or plant. Spawning follows courtship displays; fertilised eggs are placed in the nest and tended by the male. Eggs generally hatch within 24–48 hours; fry tend to become free-swimming after a further 3–4 days. A tight-fitting cover is important during the fry stage for labyrinth organ development. The female should generally be removed or given ample cover after spawning. First foods for fry are typically infusoria, vinegar eels, microworms, or commercial fry powder; newly hatched brine shrimp can be introduced as the fry grow. Breeding is generally considered easy within the Splendens complex once a compatible pair is established and appropriate water conditions are provided.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided size (50–60 mm SL), temperature (22–28°C), pH (5.5–7.5), hardness (18–179 ppm / approx 1–10 dGH), minimum tank footprint (45×30 cm base, ~41 L for pair), native range (Khorat Plateau, eastern Thailand; Mekong tributaries, central Laos), habitat, diet, bubble-nest breeding details, and care notes. Aquarium Co-Op returned 404 for this species.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Osphronemidae), order (Anabantiformes), maximum size (7.0 cm TL; consistent with SeriouslyFish 50–60 mm SL when accounting for TL vs SL difference), temperature (24–27°C; SeriouslyFish 22–28°C range preferred as it covers the full hobbyist spectrum), pH (6.0–8.0; SeriouslyFish 5.5–7.5 used as more representative of specialist husbandry), hardness (5–19 dH), native range (Mekong and Chao Phraya basins), IUCN Data Deficient status, bubble-nest reproduction, facultative air-breathing.

fish.breedingNotes: Bubble-nest builder; paternal care; male guards nest. Female should be removed or given refuge post-spawn. Fry require infusoria-grade first foods. Breeding is generally straightforward once a compatible pair and appropriate water conditions are established.
